[{"role": "system", "content": "# TASK
1. Analyze the provided labeled examples to identify the observable visual features that distinguish each class.
2. Examine the Query Image and compare it strictly against the provided examples.
3. Determine which label from the provided options best matches the Query Image.

# INSTRUCTIONS
- Base your decision only on observable visual evidence in the examples and the Query Image.
- Use the examples to infer discriminative visual patterns for each class.
- Do not use external world knowledge, hidden assumptions, or speculative attributes.
- Use only labels from the provided final options list.
- Choose exactly one final label.
- Output only the requested XML tags and not any aditional ones.
- Do not output any text outside the XML tags.
- Follow the condition-specific XML blocks first, then output exactly one final <response> block.
- The final line of the entire answer must be <response>final_class</response>.
- Never omit the final <response> block, even when another explanation block is required.

First list the critical observable features present only in the Query Image.
Then formulate a knowledge base (kb) with a small set of IF-THEN rules grounded in observable patterns from the labeled examples in the support images and the Query Image, for all classes. Try to unify the conditions so that there is only one rule per class.
Rules must refer only to visible features.
Then check which rule(s) are best satisfied by the Query Image.

<features>
- observable feature: feature value
- observable feature: feature value
- observable feature: feature value
</features>
<kb>
- IF [observable visual features = feature value AND/OR observable visual features = feature value] THEN [class1]
- IF [observable visual features = feature value] THEN [class2]
</kb>

<rule_check>
- State which rule(s) from the kb fires for the Query Image and briefly explain why,
referring only to the features listed above. Do not discuss other rules or introduce new features.
</rule_check>

<response>final_class</response>

raw_response_text:
<features>
- observable feature: flower color is pink
- observable feature: flower has multiple petals
- observable feature: flower has visible stamens
</features>
<kb>
- IF [flower color is pink AND flower has visible stamens] THEN [71]
- IF [flower is white AND has yellow center] THEN [68]
- IF [flower is pink AND has yellow center] THEN [33]
</kb>
<rule_check>
- Rule 1 fires because the flower is pink and has visible stamens, matching the features of class 71.
</rule_check>
<response>71</response>

original_messages.content:
# TASK
1. Analyze the provided labeled examples to identify the observable visual features that distinguish each class.
2. Examine the Query Image and compare it strictly against the provided examples.
3. Determine which label from the provided options best matches the Query Image.

# INSTRUCTIONS
- Base your decision only on observable visual evidence in the examples and the Query Image.
- Use the examples to infer discriminative visual patterns for each class.
- Do not use external world knowledge, hidden assumptions, or speculative attributes.
- Use only labels from the provided final options list.
- Choose exactly one final label.
- Output only the requested XML tags and not any aditional ones.
- Do not output any text outside the XML tags.
- Follow the condition-specific XML blocks first, then output exactly one final <response> block.
- The final line of the entire answer must be <response>final_class</response>.
- Never omit the final <response> block, even when another explanation block is required.

First list the critical observable features present only in the Query Image.
Then formulate a knowledge base (kb) with a small set of IF-THEN rules grounded in observable patterns from the labeled examples in the support images and the Query Image, for all classes. Try to unify the conditions so that there is only one rule per class.
Rules must refer only to visible features.
Then check which rule(s) are best satisfied by the Query Image.

<features>
- observable feature: feature value
- observable feature: feature value
- observable feature: feature value
</features>
<kb>
- IF [observable visual features = feature value AND/OR observable visual features = feature value] THEN [class1]
- IF [observable visual features = feature value] THEN [class2]
</kb>

<rule_check>
- State which rule(s) from the kb fires for the Query Image and briefly explain why,
referring only to the features listed above. Do not discuss other rules or introduce new features.
</rule_check>

<response>final_class</response>

The content of <response> must be exactly one label copied verbatim from the provided options list.

3. Determine which label from the provided options best matches the Query Image.

# INSTRUCTIONS
- Base your decision only on observable visual evidence in the examples and the Query Image.
- Use the examples to infer discriminative visual patterns for each class.
- Do not use external world knowledge, hidden assumptions, or speculative attributes.
- Use only labels from the provided final options list.
- Choose exactly one final label.
- Output only the requested XML tags and not any aditional ones.
- Do not output any text outside the XML tags.
- Follow the condition-specific XML blocks first, then output exactly one final <response> block.
- The final line of the entire answer must be <response>final_class</response>.
- Never omit the final <response> block, even when another explanation block is required.

First list the critical observable features present only in the Query Image.
Then formulate a knowledge base (kb) with a small set of IF-THEN rules grounded in observable patterns from the labeled examples in the support images and the Query Image, for all classes. Try to unify the conditions so that there is only one rule per class.
Rules must refer only to visible features.
Then check which rule(s) are best satisfied by the Query Image.

<features>
- observable feature: feature value
- observable feature: feature value
- observable feature: feature value
</features>
<kb>
- IF [observable visual features = feature value AND/OR observable visual features = feature value] THEN [class1]
- IF [observable visual features = feature value] THEN [class2]
</kb>

<rule_check>
- State which rule(s) from the kb fires for the Query Image and briefly explain why,
referring only to the features listed above. Do not discuss other rules or introduce new features.
</rule_check>

<response>final_class</response>

The content of <response> must be exactly one label copied verbatim from the provided options list.

Query Image:
Classify this image using only the labeled examples above.
Valid labels: [71, 68, 33]

message.content:
<features>
- observable feature: flower color is red
- observable feature: flower has a yellow center
- observable feature: petals are broad and slightly pointed
</features>
<kb>
- IF [flower color is red AND flower has a yellow center AND petals are broad and slightly pointed] THEN [33]
</kb>
<rule_check>
- Rule 1 fires because the Query Image shows a red flower with a yellow center and broad, slightly pointed petals, which matches the features described in the rule for class 33.
</rule_check>
<response>33</response>
